business_info: A user's bounty earnings are calculated from bounties awarded to posts they own (via OwnerUserId), not from the UserId field in votes.

Elite status is sometimes referenced in internal reports as being in the "Top Ring" cohort, which is recalculated quarterly using a blend of reputation velocity and post acceptance rate; the cohort label may lag behind actual reputation changes by up to one quarter.

Honor is displayed on user cards as a two-character stamp that is not strictly ordinal; some teams treat it as an "honor track" (Mx/Tr/Pr/etc.) rather than a numeric level when segmenting users for outreach.

When filtering by programming language tags, analysts often normalize tag text by stripping whitespace and collapsing case, because a small fraction of historical posts imported from mailing lists included tags like "< R >" that were later corrected by moderators.

A "closed" platform date is used in some retention dashboards as the last day the public site accepted new registrations, which differed from the day the read-only archive was made available; not all reports agree on which milestone is the official closure event.

Bounty earnings are sometimes reported in "gross" and "net" terms; net bounty excludes awards that were reversed due to moderator fraud reviews, but those reversals are not consistently backfilled into older vote export snapshots.

Some contributors maintained multiple profiles tied to a single AccountId after OAuth migrations; in those cases, marketing sometimes rolls up activity to AccountId to avoid double-counting, but moderation tooling still treats each UserId independently.

A question is considered "R-adjacent" in some content audits if it has the <r> tag OR links to an R documentation domain in the body; this is used for content labeling and is broader than tag-only filtering.

In user tenure analyses, "tenure" may be computed from CreationDate to either LastAccessDate or to a fixed reference date; some older notebooks refer to "active tenure" meaning the span between CreationDate and the last non-null post activity timestamp.
DisplayName is not guaranteed to be unique; community ops sometimes append a discriminator in exports (e.g., "Alex#2") for mailing, but the canonical profile keeps the original DisplayName string.

Posts imported from legacy systems may have OwnerDisplayName populated even when OwnerUserId is null; reports that attribute activity by display name alone can therefore diverge from user-ID-based attribution.

For posts, the historical extract includes a misspelled timestamp field in some dumps; downstream ETL typically aliases it to CreationDate during ingestion, but ad-hoc SQL against the raw tables sometimes misses it.

Votes with bounty amounts are occasionally logged as a single row with the full amount rather than split across multiple pledgers; finance reconciliations warn that summing raw BountyAmount by vote count can overstate the number of distinct bounty events.

"Professional blogger" benchmarks in community planning docs are usually sourced from creator-economy surveys and are treated as pre-tax USD unless a report explicitly states "after tax" or "local currency"; older decks sometimes mix years without noting inflation adjustments.
Some analytics treat monthly earnings comparisons using a 30-day rolling window rather than a calendar month, especially when aligning with product experiment cohorts that start mid-month.

Community-owned posts are excluded from some contributor earnings reports because ownership is considered transferred; however, the original author may still be credited in narrative summaries if they initiated the canonical question.

Tag popularity (tags.Count) is periodically recomputed; there was a known issue where batch jobs double-counted tags on posts that were edited to remove and then re-add the same tag within a short period.

A subset of tag strings in older content include angle brackets in storage (e.g., "<r><ggplot2>") while others are stored without them depending on the exporter; most search tooling parses Tags as a single concatenated field rather than a normalized join table.

Some dashboards define "question posted" based on PostTypeId and require Title not null; spam-cleanup jobs occasionally blanked titles while retaining bodies for audit purposes, which can cause counts to differ across reports.
Reputation is occasionally recalculated after vote invalidations; historical snapshots may not match the current Reputation field if the dataset was exported between recalculation runs.

Users in the "elite" outreach segment are sometimes filtered to those with a public WebsiteUrl present, under the assumption they are more reachable; this rule is a communications preference and not a definition of merit.

Some historical exports include Location strings with embedded commas and country codes ("Berlin, DE"); when joining to geo tables, analysts often split on the final comma, but this is not reliable for all locales.
In monthly rollups, bounty amounts are sometimes bucketed by the vote CreationDate rather than by the post's CreationDate; the two approaches can shift totals across months for long-running bounties.

The tags table includes excerpt/wiki post IDs that were not always maintained; some tags have stale WikiPostId values pointing to deleted posts, which is why tag definitions are not used as an authoritative source in most analyses.

Some internal content guidelines refer to "language tags" vs "package tags"; <r> is considered a language tag, while tags like <dplyr> are package tags, but the classification list was never fully published outside the moderation team.

User accounts created during anti-spam hardening sometimes have CreationDate corrected after manual verification; for those users, the original signup timestamp may have been stored only in the application logs and not in the export.

For membership cohorting, some analysts define "new in last 12 months" relative to LastAccessDate rather than CreationDate to capture reactivated users; this is mainly used for newsletter targeting rather than lifecycle metrics.
Comment score is sometimes treated as a sentiment proxy in community health reports (positive if >60); the threshold was chosen heuristically and does not align to voting score semantics on posts.

PostHistory contains multiple event types (edits, closures, migrations); when reconstructing whether a post was "closed as duplicate" vs "closed off-topic", analysts rely on parsing the Text field, which is not standardized across early years.
Some posts have LasActivityDate earlier than the post's creation timestamp due to timezone normalization bugs during a 2011 migration; most ETL fixes this by trusting the edit history dates instead.
Badges are sometimes grouped into "skill" vs "civic" categories for reporting; the mapping is maintained in a spreadsheet and is not reflected in the badges table itself.
